narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 64% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. As a female student from an upper-middle-income family, planning to attend full-time with a White background, your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 and LSAT score of 35 are solid but sit a bit below the typical admitted student's marks of 3.21 and 36.70, respectively. Your Year 1 school decile ranking of 6 is close to average, while your Year 3 decile of 9 is notably lower than the usual 5.53, suggesting a dip in performance later on. When the model evaluated your profile, it found that your Year 3 decile had the single biggest influence, pushing the prediction strongly toward expecting a bar failure compared to students who maintained stronger rankings. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile, which also nudged the odds toward failure, followed by your family income bracket, LSAT score, racial background, undergraduate GPA, gender, and full-time status—in that order of influence. Each of these factors, in your specific case, contributed to raising the risk of failure rather than lowering it, even though your demographics align with many successful students. For instance, while being White and full-time is common among those who pass (over 80% and 90% of the dataset), your academic metrics stood out as weaker drivers pulling the prediction the wrong way. Overall, this means your later-year grades were the standout concern, making your profile riskier than the typical applicant who passes the bar. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: retaking the LSAT to aim above 37 could help recalibrate similar models, and building a stronger academic record through postgraduate courses or targeted prep might offset that Year 3 dip. Most importantly, invest in rigorous bar exam preparation programs early—they've helped many students in similar positions outperform predictions like yours. You're clearly capable, given your upper-middle background and early promise, so persistence will pay off. Keep pushing forward; reapplying after addressing these areas could change everything.
